result:
“Writing long can feel like walking across a desert with an umbrella,” the teacher said once, “but if you keep opening the umbrella even when there’s no rain, eventually the shade becomes the reason you keep walking.” I remembered that line years later, the night the city lost its electricity for six hours and the only light in my apartment came from the laptop battery I was hoarding for something “important.” Instead I used it to type a single sentence over and over: What if the only thing left to do is to keep talking to yourself until someone answers back?

That sentence is still open in an unsaved file, one of hundreds that float in the invisible directory named “misc.” They are my private archipelago of almost-essays, half-dreams, undeclared manifestos, each pretending it might someday grow up and put on shoes. Tonight I open the folder the way a gambler fans a deck: pick a card, any card. Here is an inventory of last year’s anxieties turned prose: a paragraph about how the smell of diesel reminds me of my father’s jackets; a bullet-pointed theory that every relationship is just two people negotiating how much silence they can comfortably share; a monologue spoken by a bridge that is tired of being a metaphor. I read them and feel like an archaeologist who keeps discovering his own footprints.

People sometimes ask where writers get ideas. The honest answer is that ideas arrive the way subway rats do: they were always there, gnawing behind the wall, and the wall was never as solid as you thought. You remove one brick—one ordinary Tuesday, one overheard fragment of someone else’s breakup—and suddenly the rat is sitting on your keyboard, tail curled like a question mark, demanding to be named. The real labor is not finding the rat; it is admitting you live in the same city. Once you admit that, every train delay, every siren at 3 A.M., every headline that announces the ocean now contains more plastic than fish becomes, against your will, a paragraph you have not yet written. Living becomes an involuntary first draft.

But that metaphor is already wrong. A rat is too singular. It is more accurate to say that ideas come in colonies, and they chew tunnels that connect in the dark. A dentist mentions that the jawbone can record stress the way rings record drought, and three weeks later you are Googling “tmj civil war soldiers” at 2 A.M., learning that archaeologists can tell which nineteenth-century skulls belonged to cavalry because the constant clenching of horse-riding reshapes the bone. That fact, in turn, rubs shoulders with an anecdote your cousin told at Thanksgiving: how her toddler’s favorite lullaby is the sound of the microwave because it means milk is coming. Bones, microwaves, milk, horses, lullabies—soon they form a single neural subway map, and you ride it trying to pretend you are the conductor when you are actually the fare.

Writing is the art of stopping the train mid-tunnel and announcing to the passengers that the tunnel itself is the destination. Most riders object. They have somewhere to be: a wedding, a biopsy, the first day at a new job where they still don’t know where the bathroom is. They do not want to be told that the dark window reflecting their own face is the view they paid for. Yet every so often one person looks up, nods, and stays seated even after you open the doors. That single nod is the only royalty writers ever receive, and it spends better than money because it can re-light the cabin years later when the train is out of service and sold for scrap.

I think of the people who stayed seated when I reread notebooks from the year my mother began sending photographs of her chemotherapy wigs. Each wig had a name: “Julia” was a chestnut bob that made her look like a retired ballerina teaching French to kindergarteners; “Rogue” was silver-white and streaked with cobalt, as if the X-Men had opened a salon in a resort town. She texted me these photos with captions that sounded like dating profiles. “Julia likes long walks on the beach and refuses to discuss the ending of Lost.” I laughed in the laundromat, detergent dripping from my fingers, and then I went home and wrote none of it down because I was afraid that giving the wigs sentences would make them permanent. The next year, after the all-clear, she packed Julia and Rogue into a cardboard box labeled “Halloween??” and stored them above the water heater. I still haven’t written that scene either, but the box is louder than any paragraph I have produced since.

Instead I wrote around it: an essay about the history of artificial hair, from the Egyptians’ beeswax-and-linen beards to the merkins of Restoration actors; a short story in which a woman believes her house is shrinking each time she removes her wig; a list of things that weigh approximately one pound: a bald eagle’s skeleton, a NFL football, the average human scalp. None of these pieces mentioned my mother, yet every one of them was a postcard addressed to her from the country of almost-saying. She read them all and sent back typo corrections in the margins, never asked why I kept circling the same runway. That is its own dialect of love: to let someone orbit without demanding landing rights.

The orbit widened. I began collecting stories the way children collect smoothed glass. A neighbor who had once sung backup for a famous crooner told me that every night before showtime the singer ate exactly half a turkey sandwich and then kissed each member of the band on the forehead, a ritual none of them understood but no one refused. A barista with a degree in theoretical physics explained that the steam wand on the espresso machine produced turbulence statistically identical to the cosmic web of galaxies, only scaled to the size of a ceramic cup. A stranger on the late bus confessed that after his divorce he slept in a hammock strung between two coat racks because the sway reminded him of being eight and sick, rocked to sleep by his father pacing the living room. Each story felt like a borrowed coat: I wore it home and hung it behind the door, unsure whom it actually fit. Eventually the coats outnumbered the hooks; they piled on chairs, formed a second skin of strangers’ warmth. One night I realized I was writing less and less because I was afraid of choosing one coat and thereby betraying the rest. The fear felt like standing in front of an open refrigerator, hungry but unable to pick a single item because eating any of it would be an admission that the others would spoil.

The refrigerator metaphor is tired, but metaphors are like house keys: you keep using the jagged one even when the lock is loose because habit fits your hand better than novelty. I wonder how many sentences I have written whose only crime is that they arrived too late to the party and found all the clever comparisons already drunk on the couch. Still, I let them stay; eviction feels worse than clutter. William Stafford used to say that if you get stuck while writing, lower your standards and keep going. I picture the standards as a limbo bar that begins the night at a dignified height and by 3 A.M. is lying flat on the ground while the sentences army-crawl beneath it, uniforms filthy but still advancing. The trick is not to raise the bar again too soon, or you will decapitate the very line you need to finish the march.

Marching metaphors, war metaphors: we cannot seem to describe creativity without invoking battle, as if every act of imagination were a siege against an enemy. Maybe that is because we inherit syntax the way medieval towns inherited walls. The sentences stand guard, convinced that beyond them lies the ungovernable dark where adjectives desert and verbs conjugate in outlawed tenses. Yet the longer I write, the less I believe in the siege. The page is not a fortress; it is a truce flag. When I allow the flag to be just cloth, ink bleeds through and outlines the bodies no one agreed to count. Those bodies keep breathing once the treaty is signed. Sometimes I meet them years later in the margin of a book I had forgotten I owned: “This is the year I stopped flinching at ambulance sounds.” The handwriting is mine, but the person is a stranger I once promised to remember on behalf of.

Memory, too, is a borrowed coat. The pockets contain receipts for meals I cannot taste again, ticket stubs for trains that now run on new schedules. Yet the coat itself has molded to my shoulders; taking it off would be colder than wearing it. I think of my grandfather who kept every calendar his insurance company mailed him, thirty-seven years of small grids annotated in a shorthand only he spoke: “Froze 4 inches, chimney quiet.” After he died we found them stacked in a closet like brittle, unbound books. No one could decide whether to recycle them. In the end my cousin scanned every page and stored the files on a flash drive shaped like a gold bullion bar, a joke too literal to be funny. We are all archivists of what no one else will curate.

The calendars remind me that granularity is a form of hope. Recording daily temperatures, cataloguing wig names, noting which microwave button delivers thirty seconds versus ninety: these are ways of insisting that the world can be held in increments small enough to pocket. The terror arrives when the increments outnumber the pockets. You wake one day to discover you have seventeen different passwords, three streaming subscriptions you forgot to cancel, and a plant that has not been watered since the last presidential administration. The self fragments into administrative sub-selves who never meet. They leave Post-it notes for one another in a kitchen that now feels like a shared sublet. “Remember to vote.” “Remember the name of the medication that begins with L.” “Remember you once wanted to be a person who danced in public without needing to be good at it.” The notes yellow and curl. Dancing falls lower on the algorithm.

Against that fragmentation, writing offers an illusion of reassembly. You sit at the table and announce, I am the one who decides which version of me gets to speak right now. The others can wait in the hallway, tapping their feet. For the length of a battery, a candle, a pot of coffee, you pretend the self is monarchical rather than parliamentary. The pretense is exhausting, which is why first drafts smell like sweat and good espresso. Revision, on the other hand, smells like rain on hot asphalt: the moment when the city agrees to begin again without admitting it has changed. You walk outside and the pavement steams, and for one block you believe coherence is possible.

I have lived in seven apartments in the last twelve years. Each time I move, I pack a small metal box that contains nothing but failed beginnings: a novel outline shaped like a spiral galaxy, a play whose characters are named after bones in the ear, a stack of postcards I wrote to my future self but never mailed because the stamp price increased and I took it as an omen. The box is heavier than anything else I carry, disproportionate to its size. Movers assume it is cookware. I let them believe it; cookware at least has a recognizable function. When I unpack, I place the box on the highest shelf, unreachable without a chair. The unreachability is a superstition: if I ever open it again, I will have to finish what I started, and finishing is a form of burial. Some nights I stand on the chair anyway, fingertips grazing the cool lid, listening to the faint rustle of sentences inside like moths awake at noon.

The unfinished projects teach me that creative impulse is not a finite resource but a renewable one that regenerates according to its own lunar logic. Abandoning a piece does not amputate; it prunes. The next branch grows weirder fruit, sometimes sweeter, sometimes inedible. The gardener in me learns patience; the scavenger in me learns to collect the rotted fruit for seeds. Either way, nothing is wasted except the illusion that anything was ever mine to begin with. I speak in plant metaphors because my father kept a garden the way other men keep diaries. He recorded rainfall on a ruler screwed to a fence post, noted which tomatoes “sulked” after transplantation. When the city widened the alley and cut the roots of his peach tree, he took a photograph of the stump and framed it above the workbench. The frame still hangs there though the house belongs to strangers now. Strangers who, I like to imagine, look at the stump and assume it is art.

Art is what we call the stump when we can’t bear to call it removed. The same way we call a breakup “distance” or a layoff “restructuring.” Language loans us gentler shapes for what has been amputated. Yet the loan collects interest; each euphemism makes the next loss harder to pronounce. I try to practice naming things precisely: the peach tree is dead, the novel is stuck, the friend is not calling back. The precision hurts like cold water on a tooth that needs a root canal, but the ache maps the exact location of salvage. The dentist says, If it hurts when I tap here, raise your hand. I raise my hand so often these days it looks like I am conducting an orchestra of minor griefs. The music is ugly but at least it is audible.

Audible is what I crave when the newsfeed turns into a silent auction of disasters. Wildfires, coups, children in classrooms practicing how to barricade doors. The mind scrolls until it develops a vertigo indistinguishable from boredom. I close the app and open a blank document, not because writing will fix the fire but because syntax is still a form of citizenship. To arrange subject next to verb is to insist that cause might still yield effect, that proximity matters, that a sentence can choose to end before it causes collateral damage. The page is the one republic where I get to vote on what happens next, and even if the vote is overturned in revision, the moment of casting it is real. I保存 the file with a time stamp, a tiny treaty with ephemera.

Ephemera used to embarrass me. I wanted monuments, books you could emboss in gold foil, tombstones shaped like hardcovers. Then I worked for a year in a library’s conservation lab, rehousing nineteenth-century theater programs so acidic they crumbled like ash. Each playbill announced a night that happened once: one performance of Macbeth where the actor’s cane snapped, one matinee where a child in the balcony dropped a lemon drop onto Lady Macbeth’s crown. No archive can preserve the gasp that rose when the cane broke; it can only preserve the paper that absorbed the breath. Holding the brittle programs, I understood that ephemera is not the opposite of permanence but its shadow: proof that something was alive enough to vanish.

Vanishing tricks interest me more than appearances. I keep a list of methods people have used to erase themselves: the poet who published under seventeen anagrams and then denied them all; the painter who scraped every canvas the night before the opening; the programmer who released a virus that rewrote its own code until nothing remained but a cursor blinking the word “maybe.” The list grows longer than any poem I have managed to finish. Maybe the list is the poem, a cento of withdrawal. Or maybe it is just a mirror I hold up to my own fear that making anything solid will trap me inside it. The fear is not irrational; every book is a door that only opens inward. Once you walk through, the room rearranges and the knob disappears. You write your way out by writing another door, but the exit always leads to a hallway lined with mirrors reflecting the first room. The infinity is claustrophobic unless you decide to like hallways.

Hallways are underrated. They contain the towel closet, the mirror that shows you your profile instead of your face, the faint smell of the downstairs neighbor’s curry slipping under the door. In other words, they are the place where private and public negotiate without declaration. Most of life happens in hallways: the minute before the job interview, the minute after the kiss you weren’t sure you wanted. I try to write hallway sentences—lines that do not announce climax but rather the scent of someone else’s dinner on your clothes. The trick is to resist the temptation to open the next door too quickly. Stay in the hallway long enough to notice which lightbulb flickers like a Morse code you once knew by heart.

Hearts, like hallways, are lined with doors we pretend are walls. I discovered this when I began translating my own pulse into meter. Ten beats per line became a decasyllabic fixation; every walk to the corner store turned into scansion. The body refuses iamb for long—it wants syncopation, arrhythmia, the skipped beat that reminds you the conductor is human. I let the poem falter the way my father’s peach tree faltered when the city arrived with chainsaws. The poem drops its fruit early; the fruit stains the sidewalk; the stain looks like a map of the city before the grid was imposed. Children step over it on the way to school and unknowingly carry pieces of the map on their soles. Weeks later their mothers find dark smudges on the carpet and blame mud. The whole town becomes a palimpsest of directions to a place that no longer exists.

Existence, I am learning, is mostly aftermath. We live in the comma after the apocalypse, the pause that wonders whether the sentence will continue or the speaker merely caught a breath. The trick is to treat the comma as home: furnish it with scavenged chairs, invite neighbors to share the uncertain clause. One brings a radio that only receives stations from the previous decade; another brings a board game missing half its pieces. We reinvent rules that require improvisation: a pawn can become a queen if everyone agrees to forget what the pawn once represented. The forgetting is not denial but collage, the way a storm relocates rooftops and suddenly the sky has a new window. Through it we glimpse constellations we lacked the perspective to notice before the roof blew off.

Off is the direction I most often want to go, until I arrive and discover “off” is just another switch labeled “on” in a darker room. The revelation should be discouraging, yet it thrills me. It means there is no exit from the maze, only further corridors, and the corridors are made of the same muscle as the heart. Pulse is both map and territory. I walk until the walking becomes a sentence I can live inside, a grammatical burrow where subject and object agree to share the blanket of verb. The agreement is temporary; grammar, like weather, is a negotiation, never a contract. Still, the blanket warms enough to let me nap, and in the nap I dream of a library where every book I abandoned has been rebound in skin I recognize as my own. The spines are soft as eyelids. When I open them, they read me instead. They say: We never asked to be finished, only held long enough to know your temperature.

Temperature is how I measure time now. The year my mother wore Julia and Rogue, the thermostat hovered at seventy-two because chemotherapy makes you cold even in July. The year I moved to the city without her, the radiator clanked at night like a typewriter carriage return, and I learned to sleep through the noise by dreaming of paragraphs. This year the planet registers another fever, and I open windows at odd hours to let the night braid its cooler air through the rooms. The braid is uneven; some strands unravel into wildfire smoke, others carry the salt of an ocean I have never seen. I breathe both and call the cough a form of composition, each exhale a sentence that begins with “Suppose we survive this…” Survival is not a heroic arc; it is a series of small, awkward edits: adding a scarf, deleting a commute, inserting the word “yet” at the end of every prognosis.

Prognosis, the doctors say, is most accurate in hindsight. They might as well be describing revision. You only know which scenes were necessary once the narrative arrives at its closing image, and even then the image keeps shifting like a Polaroid developing in reverse. I keep an envelope of old photographs that lost their captions. Faces squint against sunlight I cannot date. One shows a child balanced on a bicycle two sizes too large; another captures someone’s back as they walk down a gravel road. I study them the way scholars study marginalia, hoping to decipher what urgency made someone press the shutter at that exact second. The envelope becomes a book I did not mean to write: a novel told only by what the eye insists on framing when memory has already walked out of the shot.

Shooting, in the photographic sense, is the opposite of shooting in the ballistic sense, yet the same verb carries both histories. Language is full of such double agents. I keep a private CIA of etymologies, tracking which words once meant love and now mean war, which once meant servant and now mean software. The surveillance comforts me; if I can chart the mutation, maybe I can predict which current affection will turn radioactive by the time my niece is grown. I write her letters she will not read until she learns to sound out syllables longer than my name. In them I describe everyday miracles—how rainwater beads on basil leaves like smaller planets, how the subway singer’s voice cracks exactly on the word “belong”—and I sign each letter with the date and the number of days since I last forgot to be grateful. The tally embarrasses me, but embarrassment is just love wearing the wrong shoes to the party.

Parties, I finally understand, are improvised shelters. We gather in dim rooms, trade stories like blankets, pretend the music is loud enough to muffle the sound of glaciers calving. Someone passes around a phone showing footage of ordinary people rushing into surf to rescue strangers caught in a riptide. The room hushes; we witness the moment when human limbs form a chain stronger than the current. No one speaks for a long beat, then someone laughs because the next clip autoloads a cat falling off a windowsill. The laughter is not relief but continuation, the way a heartbeat continues even when the body is asleep. I store the moment in the coat closet of memory, hang it between the scarf that smells of campfire and the jacket still flecked with someone else’s glitter. Years from now I will reach in, and the moment will fall out like a theater ticket stub: evidence that I was here, that I believed being here mattered, that I still do.

The precision hurts like cold water on a tooth that needs a root canal, but the ache maps the exact location of salvage. The dentist says, If it hurts when I tap here, raise your hand. I raise my hand so often these days it looks like I am conducting an orchestra of minor griefs. The music is ugly but at least it is audible.

Audible is what I crave when the newsfeed turns into a silent auction of disasters. Wildfires, coups, children in classrooms practicing how to barricade doors. The mind scrolls until it develops a vertigo indistinguishable from boredom. I close the app and open a blank document, not because writing will fix the fire but because syntax is still a form of citizenship. To arrange subject next to verb is to insist that cause might still yield effect, that proximity matters, that a sentence can choose to end before it causes collateral damage. The page is the one republic where I get to vote on what happens next, and even if the vote is overturned in revision, the moment of casting it is real. I保存 the file with a time stamp, a tiny treaty with ephemera.

Ephemera used to embarrass me. I wanted monuments, books you could emboss in gold foil, tombstones shaped like hardcovers. Then I worked for a year in a library’s conservation lab, rehousing nineteenth-century theater programs so acidic they crumbled like ash. Each playbill announced a night that happened once: one performance of Macbeth where the actor’s cane snapped, one matinee where a child in the balcony dropped a lemon drop onto Lady Macbeth’s crown. No archive can preserve the gasp that rose when the cane broke; it can only preserve the paper that absorbed the breath. Holding the brittle programs, I understood that ephemera is not the opposite of permanence but its shadow: proof that something was alive enough to vanish.

Vanishing tricks interest me more than appearances. I keep a list of methods people have used to erase themselves: the poet who published under seventeen anagrams and then denied them all; the painter who scraped every canvas the night before the opening; the programmer who released a virus that rewrote its own code until nothing remained but a cursor blinking the word “maybe.” The list grows longer than any poem I have managed to finish. Maybe the list is the poem, a cento of withdrawal. Or maybe it is just a mirror I hold up to my own fear that making anything solid will trap me inside it. The fear is not irrational; every book is a door that only opens inward. Once you walk through, the room rearranges and the knob disappears. You write your way out by writing another door, but the exit always leads to a hallway lined with mirrors reflecting the first room. The infinity is claustrophobic unless you decide to like hallways.

Hallways are underrated. They contain the towel closet, the mirror that shows you your profile instead of your face, the faint smell of the downstairs neighbor’s curry slipping under the door. In other words, they are the place where private and public negotiate without declaration. Most of life happens in hallways: the minute before the job interview, the minute after the kiss you weren’t sure you wanted. I try to write hallway sentences—lines that do not announce climax but rather the scent of someone else’s dinner on your clothes. The trick is to resist the temptation to open the next door too quickly. Stay in the hallway long enough to notice which lightbulb flickers like a Morse code you once knew by heart.

Hearts, like hallways, are lined with doors we pretend are walls. I discovered this when I began translating my own pulse into meter. Ten beats per line became a decasyllabic fixation; every walk to the corner store turned into scansion. The body refuses iamb for long—it wants syncopation, arrhythmia, the skipped beat that reminds you the conductor is human. I let the poem falter the way my father’s peach tree faltered when the city arrived with chainsaws. The poem drops its fruit early; the fruit stains the sidewalk; the stain looks like a map of the city before the grid was imposed. Children step over it on the way to school and unknowingly carry pieces of the map on their soles. Weeks later their mothers find dark smudges on the carpet and blame mud. The whole town becomes a palimpsest of directions to a place that no longer exists.

Existence, I am learning, is mostly aftermath. We live in the comma after the apocalypse, the pause that wonders whether the sentence will continue or the speaker merely caught a breath. The trick is to treat the comma as home: furnish it with scavenged chairs, invite neighbors to share the uncertain clause. One brings a radio that only receives stations from the previous decade; another brings a board game missing half its pieces. We reinvent rules that require improvisation: a pawn can become a queen if everyone agrees to forget what the pawn once represented. The forgetting is not denial but collage, the way a storm relocates rooftops and suddenly the sky has a new window. Through it we glimpse constellations we lacked the perspective to notice before the roof blew off.

Off is the direction I most often want to go, until I arrive and discover “off” is just another switch labeled “on” in a darker room. The revelation should be discouraging, yet it thrills me. It means there is no exit from the maze, only further corridors, and the corridors are made of the same muscle as the heart. Pulse is both map and territory. I walk until the walking becomes a sentence I can live inside, a grammatical burrow where subject and object agree to share the blanket of verb. The agreement is temporary; grammar, like weather, is a negotiation, never a contract. Still, the blanket warms enough to let me nap, and in the nap I dream of a library where every book I abandoned has been rebound in skin I recognize as my own. The spines are soft as eyelids. When I open them, they read me instead. They say: We never asked to be finished, only held long enough to know your temperature.

Temperature is how I measure time now. The year my mother wore Julia and Rogue, the thermostat hovered at seventy-two because chemotherapy makes you cold even in July. The year I moved to the city without her, the radiator clanked at night like a typewriter carriage return, and I learned to sleep through the noise by dreaming of paragraphs. This year the planet registers another fever, and I open windows at odd hours to let the night braid its cooler air through the rooms. The braid is uneven; some strands unravel into wildfire smoke, others carry the salt of an ocean I have never seen. I breathe both and call the cough a form of composition, each exhale a sentence that begins with “Suppose we survive this…” Survival is not a heroic arc; it is a series of small, awkward edits: adding a scarf, deleting a commute, inserting the word “yet” at the end of every prognosis.

Prognosis, the doctors say, is most accurate in hindsight. They might as well be describing revision. You only know which scenes were necessary once the narrative arrives at its closing image, and even then the image keeps shifting like a Polaroid developing in reverse. I keep an envelope of old photographs that lost their captions. Faces squint against sunlight I cannot date. One shows a child balanced on a bicycle two sizes too large; another captures someone’s back as they walk down a gravel road. I study them the way scholars study marginalia, hoping to decipher what urgency made someone press the shutter at that exact second. The envelope becomes a book I did not mean to write: a novel told only by what the eye insists on framing when memory has already walked out of the shot.

Shooting, in the photographic sense, is the opposite of shooting in the ballistic sense, yet the same verb carries both histories. Language is full of such double agents. I keep a private CIA of etymologies, tracking which words once meant love and now mean war, which once meant servant and now mean software. The surveillance comforts me; if I can chart the mutation, maybe I can predict which current affection will turn radioactive by the time my niece is grown. I write her letters she will not read until she learns to sound out syllables longer than my name. In them I describe everyday miracles—how rainwater beads on basil leaves like smaller planets, how the subway singer’s voice cracks exactly on the word “belong”—and I sign each letter with the date and the number of days since I last forgot to be grateful. The tally embarrasses me, but embarrassment is just love wearing the wrong shoes to the party.

Parties, I finally understand, are improvised shelters. We gather in dim rooms, trade stories like blankets, pretend the music is loud enough to muffle the sound of glaciers calving. Someone passes around a phone showing footage of ordinary people rushing into surf to rescue strangers caught in a riptide. The room hushes; we witness the moment when human limbs form a chain stronger than the current. No one speaks for a long beat, then someone laughs because the next clip autoloads a cat falling off a windowsill. The laughter is not relief but continuation, the way a heartbeat continues even when the body is asleep. I store the moment in the coat closet of memory, hang it between the scarf that smells of campfire and the jacket still flecked with someone else’s glitter. Years from now I will reach in, and the moment will fall out like a theater ticket stub: evidence that I was here, that I believed being here mattered, that I still do.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
